target.name: peyssonnosol
target.motivation: Peyssonnosol (3) is the aglycone of peyssonnoside A (1), a marine red algal diterpene with pronounced antifungal activity and activity against Plasmodium berghei. Its unique tetracyclic skeleton and bioactivity make it an interesting subject of study. While total syntheses exist, the biosynthetic pathway and the terpene synthase (DTS) responsible were unknown.

success_criteria.free_text: Identification and mechanistic characterization of diterpene synthases (DTSs) for peyssonnosol (3) and its regioisomer peyssonnosol B (4), including elucidation of their enzyme mechanisms through isotopic labelling, DFT calculations, and site-directed mutagenesis.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: This paper focuses on the biosynthesis of peyssonnosol, not a chemical total synthesis. The 'retrosynthetic strategy' here refers to the proposed biosynthetic pathways, which are essentially forward reaction cascades catalyzed by enzymes. The primary goal is to understand the enzyme mechanism, not to synthesize the molecule from simple precursors in a lab setting.

thought.background: Peyssonnosides A (1) and B (2) and their aglycone peyssonnosol (3) have been discovered from a marine red alga. Total syntheses of peyssonnosol (3) and peyssonnosol B (4) have been reported, but the diterpene synthase (DTS) for 3 is currently unknown.
thought.gap: The biosynthetic pathway and the specific DTS enzymes responsible for peyssonnosol (3) and peyssonnosol B (4) are unknown.
thought.decision: To identify DTSs for peyssonnosol and peyssonnosol B, a functional screening of bacterial terpene synthase homologs will be performed.

outcome.observation: Two DTSs were discovered during a functional screening of 312 bacterial TS homologs. These DTSs are phylogenetically distant from other characterized bacterial enzymes.

evidence.text_span:
Recently, the structurally remarkable peyssonnosides A (1) and B (2) have been discovered from a marine red alga attributed to the genus Peyssonnelia (Figure 1).[16] In addition to their unique 6–3–6–5 tetracyclic skeleton with two adjacent quaternary centres embedded in a cyclopropane motif, these sulphated diterpene glycosides show a pronounced antifungal activity and are active against Plasmodium berghei. Peyssonnoside A and its aglycone peyssonnosol (3), a putative DTS product, have been accessed through total synthesis,[17,18] but a DTS for 3 is currently unknown. Here, we report on the identification and mechanistic characterisation of two DTSs for 3 and its regioisomer peyssonnosol B (4).
During a functional screening of 312 bacterial TS homologs, two DTSs were discovered that are phylogenetically distant to other characterised bacterial enzymes (Figure S1).

thought.background: Two DTSs were identified. The first enzyme is from Anaerolineae bacterium HKST-UBA68.
thought.gap: The product of the first DTS needs to be identified.
thought.decision: To identify the product, heterologous overexpression of ERG20F96C (geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate synthase, GGPPS) and the DTS will be performed in engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae YZL141 for enhanced terpene precursor supply.

outcome.observation: The diterpene alcohol peyssonnosol (3) was isolated.

evidence.text_span: The function of the first enzyme from Anaerolineae bacterium HKST-UBA68 was investigated through heterologous overexpression of ERG20F96C (geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate synthase, GGPPS)[21] and the DTS in the engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae YZL141 for an enhanced terpene precursor supply,[22] allowing for the isolation of the diterpene alcohol peyssonnosol (3) (Figures 1, S2–S9, Table S2).

thought.background: The first DTS produced peyssonnosol (3). The second DTS is from Anaerolineaceae bacterium NAT117.
thought.gap: The product of the second DTS needs to be identified.
thought.decision: To identify the product, the same approach as for the first DTS will be used: heterologous overexpression of ERG20F96C and the DTS in engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae YZL141.

outcome.observation: The product of the second DTS was identified as peyssonnosol B (4).

evidence.text_span: Using the same approach, the product of the second DTS from Anaerolineaceae bacterium NAT117 was identified as peyssonnosol B (4) (Table S3, Figures S10–S17).

thought.background: Peyssonnosol (3) and peyssonnosol B (4) were identified as products of AbPS1 and AbPS2, respectively.
thought.gap: The absolute configuration of the bacterial products needs to be confirmed and compared to previously reported synthetic compounds.
thought.decision: To determine the absolute configuration, optical rotation comparison with synthetic 3 and anomalous dispersion (Cu-Kα) X-ray crystallography for both 3 and 4 will be used.

outcome.observation: The absolute configuration of bacterial 3 was identical to the synthetic compound. This was confirmed by X-ray crystallography for both 3 and 4.
outcome.specific_rotation: [α]D^25 = -24.8 (c 0.58, CH2Cl2) for bacterial 3, compared to [α]D^22 = -31.2 (c 0.3, CHCl3) for synthetic 3.

evidence.text_span: The absolute configuration of 3 was assigned by comparison of the optical rotation ( [α]D^25 = -24.8 , c 0.58, CH2Cl2 ) to data for the synthetic compound ( [α]D^22 = -31.2 , c 0.3, CHCl3 , ),[18] revealing identical absolute configurations for bacterial and algal 3. This was confirmed through anomalous dispersion (Cu-Kα) X-ray cyrstallography for both compounds 3 and 4 (Scheme 1, Figures S18 and S19, Tables S4 and S5).

thought.background: AbPS1 and AbPS2 produce 3 and 4 from GGPP in vivo. The substrate specificity needs to be confirmed in vitro.
thought.gap: Determine which prenyl pyrophosphate substrates (GPP, FPP, GGPP, GFPP) are accepted by the recombinant enzymes.
thought.decision: To investigate substrate specificity, recombinant AbPS1 and AbPS2 will be expressed in E. coli, purified, and incubated with various prenyl pyrophosphates.

outcome.observation: Both AbPS1 and AbPS2 efficiently converted GGPP into 3 and 4, respectively. Other substrates (GPP, FPP, GFPP) were not accepted.

evidence.text_span: The recombinant enzymes AbPS1 and AbPS2 (PeyS) were made accessible through heterologous expression in Escherichia coli BL21(DE3), purified (Figure S20) and incubated with geranyl (GPP), farnesyl (FPP), geranylgeranyl (GGPP) and geranylfarnesyl pyrophosphate (GFPP), showing the efficient conversion of GGPP into 3 and 4, respectively, while all other substrates were not accepted (Figures S21 and S22).

thought.background: AbPS1 and AbPS2 convert GGPP to 3 and 4. A biosynthetic proposal for 3 by Kubanek exists (Scheme S1).
thought.gap: A detailed mechanistic proposal for the cyclization cascade from GGPP to 3 and 4 is needed, incorporating known stereochemical information.
thought.decision: To formulate a mechanistic hypothesis, starting with a specific GGPP conformation and proposing a series of cyclizations, hydride shifts, and methyl migrations, leading to 3 and 4.

outcome.observation: A first hypothesis was formulated, starting with a GGPP conformation, followed by 1,11-cyclisation to B, 10,14-cyclisation to C, two 1,2-hydride shifts to D and E, a 1,2-Me group migration to F, deprotonation to H, reprotonation to I, cyclisation to J, 1,2-hydride shift to K, cyclopropanation to L, and finally water capture to 4 or another 1,2-hydride shift to M for 3.
outcome.free_text: This mechanism was supported by extensive isotopic labelling experiments.

evidence.text_span: A biosynthetic proposal for 3 by Kubanek (Scheme S1)[16] was refined during the course of this study (Scheme 1). Our first hypothesis starts with a GGPP conformation with Me18 and Me19 pointing down and Me20 up. The same conformation can be adopted by (R)-geranyllinalyl pyrophosphate (GLPP), but not by (S)-GLPP, assuming an anti-SN2' reaction in the initial 1,11-cyclisation to B. This was confirmed through the incubation of both enantiomers of GLPP[30] with AbPS1, showing a higher efficiency for the conversion of (R)-GLPP into 3 than for (S)-GLPP (Figure S27). A second 10,14- cyclisation to C is followed by two 1,2-hydride shifts to D and E and a 1,2-Me group migration to F. Its deprotonation may directly lead to H, and a reprotonation at C3 can yield I with a cisoid allyl system that sets the stage for the cyclisation to J. A subsequent 1,2-hydride shift to K enables cyclopropanation to L and water capture to 4. Alternatively, L can undergo another 1,2-hydride shift to M as the precursor of 3.

thought.background: The first mechanistic hypothesis suggests (R)-GLPP as a possible intermediate.
thought.gap: Confirm the preference for (R)-GLPP over (S)-GLPP in the AbPS1 catalyzed reaction.
thought.decision: To confirm the stereoselectivity, both enantiomers of GLPP will be incubated with AbPS1.

outcome.observation: Higher efficiency for the conversion of (R)-GLPP into 3 than for (S)-GLPP was observed.

evidence.text_span: This was confirmed through the incubation of both enantiomers of GLPP[30] with AbPS1, showing a higher efficiency for the conversion of (R)-GLPP into 3 than for (S)-GLPP (Figure S27).

thought.background: The first mechanistic hypothesis involves several hydride and methyl shifts, and deprotonation/reprotonation steps. Isotopic labelling experiments can provide evidence for these steps.
thought.gap: Confirm the proposed 1,2-hydride shift from C to D.
thought.decision: To investigate the 1,2-hydride shift from C to D, (3-13C, 2-2H)DMAPP will be used in conjunction with IPP, GGPPS, and AbPS1.

outcome.observation: The obtained product exhibited an upfield shifted triplet for C7 of 3 (Δδ = -0.54 ppm, 1JC,D = 19.1 Hz), confirming a covalent 13C-2H bond in the product.

evidence.text_span: The 1,2-hydride shift from C to D was investigated using (3-13C, 2-2H)DMAPP[35] in conjunction with IPP, GGPPS and AbPS1. The obtained product exhibited an upfield shifted triplet for C7 of 3 Δδ = -0.54 ppm , 1JC,D = 19.1 Hz ), confirming a covalent 13C2H bond in the product (Figure S31).

thought.background: Evidence for the 1,2-hydride shift from C to D was obtained.
thought.gap: Confirm the proposed 1,2-hydride shift from D to E.
thought.decision: To investigate the 1,2-hydride shift from D to E, (2-13C)DMAPP and (2,2-2H2)IPP will be used with GGPPS and AbPS1.

outcome.observation: The experiment showed a Δδ = -0.64 ppm, 1JC,D = 19.1 Hz, supporting the 1,2-hydride shift from D to E.

evidence.text_span: Employing the same strategy, the 1,2-hydride shift from D to E was shown through incubation of (2-13C)DMAPP[36] and (2,2-2H2)IPP (synthesised as in Scheme S2) with GGPPS and AbPS1 ( Δδ = -0.64 ppm , 1JC,D = 19.1 Hz , Figure S32).

thought.background: Hydride shifts from C to D and D to E were confirmed.
thought.gap: Investigate the deprotonation-reprotonation sequence G-H-I.
thought.decision: To investigate the G-H-I sequence, (S)-(1-13C, 1-2H)GGPP (prepared from FPP and (S)-(1-13C, 1-2H)IPP) will be converted by AbPS1.

outcome.observation: An upfield shifted triplet for C1 (Δδ = -0.38 ppm, 1JC,D = 23.7 Hz) was observed, confirming that the 1-pro-S hydrogen of GGPP stays at C1.

evidence.text_span: The deprotonation-reprotonation sequence G-H-I was investigated through several experiments. The AbPS1 catalysed conversion of (S)-(1-13C, 1-2H)GGPP, prepared with GGPPS from FPP and (S)-(1-13C, 1-2H)IPP, resulted in an upfield shifted triplet for C1 Δδ = -0.38 ppm , 1JC,D = 23.7 Hz )), confirming that the 1-pro-S hydrogen of GGPP stays at C1 (Figure S33).

thought.background: The 1-pro-S hydrogen of GGPP stays at C1 during the G-H-I sequence.
thought.gap: Determine the fate of the 1-pro-R hydrogen of GGPP during the G-H-I sequence.
thought.decision: To investigate the 1-pro-R hydrogen, an analogous experiment with (R)-(1-13C, 1-2H)IPP will be performed.

outcome.observation: Two singlet signals for C1 were produced, one matching unlabelled 3 (loss of 1-pro-R hydrogen), and a second with a small upfield shift (Δδ = -0.10 ppm) indicating deuterium at a neighboring position (C2). This suggests partial reincorporation of the abstracted proton at C3 in I, and a later 1,2-hydride shift from L to M defines its target position at C2.

evidence.text_span: The analogous experiment with (R)–(1-13C, 1-2H)IPP produced two singlet signals for C1, one of which showed the exact same chemical shift as C1 of unlabelled 3, in agreement with loss of the 1-pro-R hydrogen of GGPP. The second singlet exhibited a small upfield shift ( Δδ = -0.10 ppm )), which is typical for a deuterium residing in a neighbouring position (C2). This observation is explainable by a partial reincorporation of the same proton that is abstracted from C1 at C3 in I, and the later 1,2-hydride shift from L to M defines its target position at C2.

thought.background: The deprotonation-reprotonation sequence G-H-I involves proton abstraction and reincorporation.
thought.gap: Confirm the position of deuterium incorporation from the medium and the remaining 1-pro-R hydrogen.
thought.decision: To confirm deuterium incorporation, (2-13C)GGPP will be converted in D2O buffer. To confirm the fate of the 1-pro-R hydrogen, FPP and (2-13C, 1,1-2H2)DMAPP will be converted with IDI, GGPPS, and AbPS1.

outcome.observation: Partial deuterium incorporation from D2O was localized at C2 (Δδ = -0.41 ppm, 1JC,D = 19.1 Hz). The remaining 1-pro-R hydrogen was also localized at C2 (Δδ = -0.54 ppm, 1JC,D = 19.4 Hz).

evidence.text_span: In a complementary experiment with (2-13C)GGPP in D2O buffer, the partial deuterium incorporation from the medium was localised at C2 Δδ = -0.41 ppm , 1JC,D = 19.1 : Hz , Figure S34), while the fraction of the 1-pro-R hydrogen remaining in the molecule was likewise localised at C2 in an enzymatic transformation of FPP and (2-13C, 1,1-2H2)DMAPP[37] with IDI, GGPPS and AbPS1 ( Δδ = -0.54 ppm , 1JC,D = 19.4 : Hz , Figure S35).

thought.background: Several steps of the proposed mechanism have been supported by isotopic labelling.
thought.gap: Confirm the 1,2-hydride migration from J to K.
thought.decision: To investigate the 1,2-hydride migration from J to K, (3-13C, 2-2H)FPP and IPP will be converted with GGPPS and AbPS1.

outcome.observation: Evidence for the 1,2-hydride migration from J to K was observed (Δδ = -0.47 ppm, 1JC,D = 19.2 Hz).

evidence.text_span: Finally, the 1,2-hydride migration from J to K was evident from the conversion of (3-13C, 2-2H)FPP[38] and IPP with GGPPS and AbPS1 Δδ = -0.47 ppm , 1JC,D = 19.2 : Hz , Figure S36).

thought.background: The first mechanistic hypothesis (Scheme 1) is supported by isotopic labelling experiments.
thought.gap: Evaluate the energetic feasibility of the proposed mechanism using computational methods.
thought.decision: To assess the mechanism's feasibility, extensive DFT calculations will be performed to determine reaction barriers and Gibbs free energies for each step.

outcome.observation: Most proposed transformations showed low reaction barriers, and the overall process is strongly exergonic (-50.5 kcal mol-1). However, a high activation barrier of 20.3 kcal mol-1 was found for the 1,2-hydride shift from F to G, which is problematic.
outcome.free_text: The deprotonation-reprotonation sequence from F to I was realized using NH3 as a surrogate base. A direct transformation of F to H was not possible; instead, trials to remove H_R+ triggered a 1,2-hydride migration to G, which can then be deprotonated to H.

evidence.text_span: This mechanism was further investigated through extensive DFT calculations. Most proposed transformations showed low reaction barriers, and the overall process is with –50.5 kcalmol-1 strongly exergonic (Table S7 and Figure S37). The deprotonation-reprotonation sequence from F to I was realised using NH3 as a surrogate base.[39,40] However, a direct transformation of F to H was not possible (or at least not found computationally with the weak, but realistic surrogate base NH3 ), but instead, trials to remove HR+ triggered a 1,2-hydride migration to G, which can then be deprotonated to H (note that both hydrogens at C1 are not in hyperconjugation with the empty p-orbital in F, box in Scheme 1). A high activation barrier of 20.3 kcalmol-1 was found for the 1,2-hydride shift from F to G that results from different phenomena: First, F is a species of comparably low energy because it is well stabilised through a homoallylic cation-π interaction between C1 and C3 with a distance of 2.1 Å that needs to be broken up towards the transition state F-G-TS, and second, this transition state encounters stereoelectronic destabilisation, because of a high strain in its twisted protonated diene unit and increasing steric hindrance between Me20 and C12. The maximum difference between a local minimum and a subsequent local maximum for the whole mechanism was found to be +28.7 kcal mol-1 and is associated with this F-to-G transformation (Figure S37). For the same reasons as discussed above for this step, a similarly high barrier can be expected for the deprotonation of F by an active site residue that will cause similar molecular mechanics as the 1,2-hydride shift in F, questioning the relevance of the proposed and suggesting the need for an alternative mechanism.

thought.background: The first mechanistic hypothesis (Scheme 1) has a problematic high activation barrier for the 1,2-hydride shift from F to G, questioning its relevance.
thought.gap: The high barrier for the F to G transformation needs to be addressed, suggesting an alternative mechanism or modification.
thought.decision: The current mechanism for the F to G step is considered problematic due to high activation barrier and stereoelectronic destabilization.

outcome.observation: A high activation barrier of 20.3 kcal mol-1 was found for the 1,2-hydride shift from F to G, questioning the relevance of this step in the proposed mechanism. This suggests the need for an alternative mechanism.
outcome.free_text: The maximum difference between a local minimum and a subsequent local maximum for the whole mechanism was found to be +28.7 kcal mol-1 and is associated with this F-to-G transformation.

evidence.text_span: A high activation barrier of 20.3 kcalmol-1 was found for the 1,2-hydride shift from F to G that results from different phenomena: First, F is a species of comparably low energy because it is well stabilised through a homoallylic cation-π interaction between C1 and C3 with a distance of 2.1 Å that needs to be broken up towards the transition state F-G-TS, and second, this transition state encounters stereoelectronic destabilisation, because of a high strain in its twisted protonated diene unit and increasing steric hindrance between Me20 and C12. The maximum difference between a local minimum and a subsequent local maximum for the whole mechanism was found to be +28.7 kcal mol-1 and is associated with this F-to-G transformation (Figure S37). For the same reasons as discussed above for this step, a similarly high barrier can be expected for the deprotonation of F by an active site residue that will cause similar molecular mechanics as the 1,2-hydride shift in F, questioning the relevance of the proposed and suggesting the need for an alternative mechanism.

thought.background: Minor products of TSs can provide insights into catalytic mechanisms. Structural models of AbPS1 were generated.
thought.gap: Identify minor products and their formation pathways by modifying the enzyme active site.
thought.decision: To identify minor products, Ala scanning of hydrophobic active site residues of AbPS1 will be performed. The AbPS1-T182A variant will be investigated further.

outcome.observation: The AbPS1-T182A variant exhibited good production of a diterpene hydrocarbon, identified as peyssonnosene (5). This compound was also found in traces in the wildtype.

evidence.text_span: Most exchanges showed little effect or an abolished activity, but the AbPS1-T182A variant exhibited a good production of a diterpene hydrocarbon that was also found in traces in the wildtype. Isolation from an incubation of GGPP with the purified enzyme (Figure S41) identified this compound as peyssonnosene (5) (Table S10, Figures S42–S51).

thought.background: The AbPS1-T182A variant produced peyssonnosene (5).
thought.gap: Identify other minor products from the AbPS1-T182A variant.
thought.decision: To identify other minor products, anaerol (6) will be isolated from a production strain expressing AbPS1-T182A.

outcome.observation: Anaerol (6) was isolated from a production strain expressing AbPS1-T182A. Its absolute configuration and the loss of the 1-pro-R hydrogen were determined.

evidence.text_span: In addition, anaerol (6) was isolated from a production strain expressing AbPS1-T182A (Table S11, Figures S52–S59), and its absolute configuration and the loss of the 1-pro-R hydrogen were determined using the stereoselective deuteration strategy (Figures S60 and S61). This compound may be formed from H through reprotonation-induced water addition (Scheme 1).

thought.background: AbPS1 and AbPS2 share a common mechanism but differ in final hydroxylations. Structural models show only three different residues in the active sites.
thought.gap: Investigate the role of active site residues in product selectivity by swapping them between AbPS1 and AbPS2.
thought.decision: To test the role of active site residues, site-directed mutagenesis experiments with residue swapping will be conducted for both enzymes, creating single, double, and triple variants.

outcome.observation: Only in three cases (AbPS2-L56V, AbPS2-F75A, and AbPS2-L56V-F75A) was a partial swap of production from 4 to 3 observed, but no full functional change. The AbPS2-L56V-F75A variant showed the formation of anaerol B (7), also observed in traces in wildtype enzymes.

evidence.text_span: Despite their low sequence identity, AbPS1 and AbPS2 share a common mechanism, differing only in the final hydroxylations to 3 versus 4. A comparison of the two models docked with L revealed a high structural similarity between the two enzymes (root mean square deviation, RMSD = 0.831 ), with only three different residues in the active sites. To test their role for product selectivity, sitedirected mutagenesis experiments with residue swapping were conducted (Figure 2b,c). For both enzymes, the three single exchanges, three combinations of double exchanges and the triple variant were created. In only three cases, a partial swap of the production from 4 to 3 by AbPS2 was observed (AbPS2-L56V, AbPS2-F75A and AbPS2-L56V-F75A), but no full functional change could be achieved. Instead, the AbPS2-L56V-F75A variant showed the formation of a diterpene alcohol that was also observed in traces in the wildtype enzymes, and structurally characterised as anaerol B (7, Table S12, Figures S63–S70). Its absolute configuration was determined through stereoselective labelling experiments (Figure S71).

thought.background: The AbPS2-L56V-F75A variant produced anaerol B (7). The first mechanistic hypothesis (Scheme 1) had a problematic step (F to G).
thought.gap: Propose an alternative biosynthetic mechanism for 7 that resolves the problematic step in Scheme 1 and is consistent with observed products.
thought.decision: To explain the biosynthesis of 7, a second alternative mechanism (Scheme 2a) will be proposed, starting with a different GGPP conformation (all three Me groups pointing down), involving ionisation to A', 1,11–10,14-cyclisation to C', two 1,2-hydride shifts, a 1,2-methyl migration to F', a 1,2-hydride shift to G', deprotonation to H', reprotonation to I-, and terminal 2,6- and 1,7-cyclizations to N' and O' followed by water capture to 7.

outcome.observation: A second alternative mechanism (Scheme 2a) was proposed for the biosynthesis of 7. DFT calculations revealed low activation barriers for all elementary steps, and in particular resolved the problematic step (F to G) of Scheme 1. The corresponding 1,2-hydride shift F' to G' is not associated with steric hindrance and shows a much lower activation barrier.
outcome.free_text: A conformational change in H-, leading to intermediate H of Scheme 1, could open a pathway to 3 (Scheme 2b). However, DFT calculations revealed a high barrier for this conformational change (27.2 kcal mol-1), and the starting conformer of GGPP should be substitutable by (S)-GLPP, not (R)-GLPP, questioning the relevance of this second mechanism for 3.

evidence.text_span: The biosynthesis of 7 is explainable from GGPP with a different starting conformation as discussed in the first mechanistic hypothesis, with all three Me groups pointing down, that can explain a late-stage double cyclisation from I- to o' (Scheme 2a). After ionisation to A' a 1,11–10,14-cyclisation results in C' , and two subsequent 1,2-hydride shifts and a 1,2-methyl migration lead via D' and E' to F' . A subsequent 1,2-hydride shift to G' , deprotonation to H' and reprotonation result in I- . A terminal 2,6-cyclisation to N' , a 1,7-cyclisation to o' and capture with water yield 7. A recently proposed pathway to tetraisoquinene (8, box in Scheme 2) proceeds through similar steps to a stereoisomer of o' .[45,46] DFT calculations (Table S13, Figure S72) revealed low activation barriers for all elementary steps and in particular resolved the situation for the problematic step (F to G) of Scheme 1. The corresponding 1,2-hydride shift F' to G' (Scheme 2a) is not associated with steric hindrance causing a twisted allyl system and thus shows a much lower activation barrier. A conformational change in H- , leading to intermediate H of Scheme 1, could open a pathway to 3 (Scheme 2b). This biosynthetic scenario is also in agreement with all labelling experiments, but the DFT calculations revealed a high barrier for the conformational change ( 27.2 kcal mol-1 .). Furthermore, the starting conformer of GGPP should be substitutable by (S)- GLPP, and not (R)-GLPP, which questions the relevance of this second biosynthetic mechanism for 3.

thought.background: The first mechanism (Scheme 1) has a problematic high barrier. The second mechanism (Scheme 2) is better for 7 but less relevant for 3 due to GLPP stereoselectivity and a high conformational change barrier.
thought.gap: Propose a third alternative mechanism that is consistent with all labelling experiments, resolves the high barrier issue, and explains the observed (R)-GLPP preference for 3.
thought.decision: To address the limitations of previous mechanisms, a third alternative mechanism (Scheme 3) will be proposed, starting with isomerisation of GGPP to (R)-GLPP, followed by a conformational change, ionisation to A'', 1,11–10,14-cyclisation to C'', hydride migrations, a 1,2-Me group shift to F'' (associated with a major conformational change), deprotonation to H'', reprotonation to I'', and terminal steps to 3–5 (same as Scheme 1). Anaerol (6) formation will also be explained.

outcome.observation: A third alternative mechanism (Scheme 3) was proposed. DFT computations revealed a smooth passage with low activation barriers for all steps. Notably, deprotonation in F'' was computationally achievable without a prior 1,2-hydride shift. This mechanism is in line with all labelling experiments and explains the formation of 3, 4, 5, and 6.
outcome.free_text: This third mechanistic hypothesis currently seems to be the best model for the biosynthesis of 3 and 4.

evidence.text_span: The cyclisation mechanism of Scheme 1 explains the observed product formation from (R)-GLPP, but does not require GLPP as an intermediate, because no conformational change by rotation around the C2─C3 bond before cyclisation is considered in this pathway.[47] A third alternative mechanism that is also in line with all labelling experiments may proceed through (R)-GLPP and involve such a conformational change (Scheme 3). This pathway starts with the isomerisation of GGPP to (R)-GLPP and its conformational change by vinyl group rotation. Ionisation to A'' allows for a 1,11–10,14-cyclisation to C'' with installation of a Z -configured double bond. The hydrogens at C1 in C'' now reside in different positions as compared to the previous mechanisms. This is followed by two 1,2-hydride migrations and a 1,2-Me group shift towards F'' that is associated with a major conformational change caused by a cation-π interaction, turning Me20 from the front to the back side (Scheme 3b). The base in the deprotonationreprotonation sequence operates now on the other side of the diene portion for selective migration or removal of HR , respectively. Notably, the deprotonation in F'' with NH3 was computationally achievable without a prior 1,2-hydride shift. The reprotonation of H'' then resulted in I. The terminal steps to 3 – 5 are the same as in Scheme 1. DFT computations for this pathway (Table S14, Figure S73) revealed a smooth passage with low activation barriers for all steps, and also anaerol (6) can be explained through reprotonation of H'' to P'' and capture with water (Scheme 3).

validation.significance: This study identified two bacterial diterpene synthases (AbPS1 and AbPS2) responsible for the biosynthesis of the red algal diterpenes peyssonnosol (3) and peyssonnosol B (4). It provides a refined mechanistic model for their biosynthesis, resolving issues with previous hypotheses through extensive isotopic labelling experiments, DFT calculations, and site-directed mutagenesis. The identical absolute configurations of bacterial and algal 3 raise questions about the true biosynthetic origin of these diterpenes in Peyssonnelia, contributing to the ongoing debate about microbial symbionts versus host organisms as producers of secondary metabolites. The work highlights the complexity of terpene biosynthesis and the necessity of combining experimental and computational approaches for mechanistic elucidation.

source.title: Biosynthesis of the Red Algal Diterpene Peyssonnosol in Bacteria.
source.authors: Yin Z, Hu Z, Yang K, Lu G, Tabekoueng GB, Wu P, Luo Q, Xu J, Goldfuss B, Dickschat JS, Bian G.
